1: wayhî bᵉḥōḏeš nîsān šᵉnat ʿeśrîm: 'and it came to pass in the month Nisan in the twentieth year.' Nisan is March–April — four months after Kislev (Nehemiah 1:1), indicating Nehemiah prayed and fasted for four months before this moment. The word ḥōḏeš (month, new moon) reflects the lunar calendar. The absence of day notation for the Kislev date but its presence here likely reflects the formal court context. wayyiṭṭaḇ hayyayin lᵉp̄ānāyw: 'and wine was before him' — the cupbearer's professional duty is described with nāśāʾ (lifted, carried), establishing the scene before the king's question.
2: wayyōʾmer lî hammelek maddûaʿ pāneḵā rāʿîm: 'and the king said to me, why is your face sad?' The adjective rāʿîm (literally 'evil' — the same root as rāʿāh, evil/trouble) means here 'troubled' or 'unhappy.' A court servant showing sadness before the king was potentially dangerous — it could be interpreted as displeasure with the king. Nehemiah's response acknowledges this: wayyîrāʾ mᵉʾōḏ, 'and I was very afraid.' The fear is not cowardice but professional realism about court protocol. lōʾ-zulātî kî ʾim-raʿ lēḇ: 'unless it be evil/sadness of heart' — lēḇ (heart) points to interior motivation, the source of the visible grief.
3: haʿîr bêt qiḇrôt-ʾăḇōtay ḥārēḇāh: 'the city of my fathers' graves is desolate.' The appeal to ancestral burial sites is deeply significant in ancient Near Eastern culture — the honor due the dead was connected to maintaining the physical site. bêt qiḇrôt (house of graves) invokes both familial piety and the sacred geography of the land. The walls broken down and gates burned are the same conditions reported by Hanani in ch. Nehemiah 1:3, but now Nehemiah frames them for royal consumption: not as religious failure but as neglect of ancestral heritage — a concept any Persian king would understand.
4: wayyōʾmer lî hammelek ʿal-mah-zeh ʾattāh mᵉḇaqqēš: 'and the king said to me, what are you requesting?' The direct royal invitation to speak is the window Nehemiah sought. His response: wāʾetpallēl ʾel-ʾĕlōhê haššāmayim, 'and I prayed to the God of heaven.' This is the famous 'arrow prayer' — a silent, immediate prayer while the royal conversation was ongoing. The participial force of wayyōʾmer…wāʾetpallēl suggests simultaneous action: the king spoke and Nehemiah prayed in the same moment. The prayer is not narrated; it was the internalization of four months of intercession condensed into a breath. It represents the Nehemiah memoir's most characteristic spiritual pattern: prayer integrated into action rather than preceding it.
5: ʾim-ʿal-hammelek ṭôḇ wᵉʾim-yîṭaḇ ʿaḇdᵉḵā lᵉp̄ānêḵā: 'if it pleases the king, and if your servant has found favor in your sight.' The double politeness formula is standard Achaemenid court language, reflecting the subordination of petitioner to monarch. The specific request: tiślaḥēnî ʾel-yᵉhûḏāh ʾel-ʿîr qiḇrôt-ʾăḇōtay, 'send me to Judah, to the city of my fathers' graves.' The commissioning language šālaḥ (send) parallels YHWH's sending of prophets and Moses — in Nehemiah 2:12 Nehemiah will reflect that God put the journey on his heart. The court sending is the human instrument of the divine commission.
6: wahhašśēgāl yōšeḇet ʾeṣlô: 'and the queen was sitting beside him.' šēgāl is a rare word for queen consort (also in Psalms 45:9; Daniel 5:2), possibly a loanword from Akkadian šēgaltu. Her presence is noted as part of the narrative context but may also explain the king's more intimate and favorable reception — family settings were more conducive to personal requests. The king set a time (wayyitten-lî zᵉman), indicating a fixed term of absence. Nehemiah's answer of a specific date demonstrates advance preparation: the request was not impulsive but carefully planned during the four months of prayer.
7: ʾigᵉrôt ʾel-paḥôt ʿēḇer hannāhār: 'letters to the governors of Beyond the River.' paḥôt is the administrative title for Persian provincial governors (from Old Persian frataraka or Akkadian bēl pāḥāti). ʿēḇer hannāhār (Beyond the River / Trans-Euphrates) was the standard Persian satrapy name for the territory west of the Euphrates including Judah. The letters are safe-conduct documents (yitten-lî ʿādê ʾašer-ʾeḇôr: 'let them give me passage until I arrive'). Nehemiah's insistence on royal documentation reflects Persian bureaucratic procedure: official travel required written authorization.
8: wᵉʾigeret ʾel-ʾāsāp̄ šōmēr happardēs ʾašer lammeleḵ: 'and a letter to Asaph the keeper of the king's forest.' pardēs is a Persian loanword (Old Persian pairi-daēza, enclosed garden/park) appearing in Hebrew only here, in Ecclesiastes 2:5, and Song of Solomon 4:13. It entered Greek as paradeisos and English as 'paradise.' The king's forest provided the timber (beams/qôrôt) for the city gates, the citadel adjacent to the temple, and Nehemiah's own house. The phrase wᵉyaḏ ʾĕlōhay haṭṭôḇāh ʿālay, 'and the good hand of my God was upon me' — yaḏ (hand) as instrument of divine favor is a characteristic Ezra-Nehemiah theological formula (Ezra 7:6, 9, 28; Nehemiah 8:18, 22, 31; Nehemiah 2:18).
9: rišyôn is not an attested Hebrew word in this form — the text likely refers to the ʾigᵉrôt (letters) of Nehemiah 2:7-8, which functioned as official authorization. The word šārê hayyām (commanders of the army) indicates a military escort, consistent with Persian practice for important administrative officials traveling through potentially unstable provinces. The escort demonstrates that Nehemiah traveled with imperial backing, not as a private individual — the mission had royal weight.
10: wayyiśmaʿ sanballat haḥōrōnî wᵉṭôḇiyyāhû haʿeḇeḏ hāʿammōnî: 'Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite servant heard.' Sanballat (Sin-uballiṭ = 'the moon-god gives life') was the governor of Samaria; his Babylonian theophoric name indicates the syncretistic religious environment of the post-exilic region. A papyrus from Elephantine (408 BCE) confirms a Sanballat as governor of Samaria with sons named Delaiah and Shelemiah — Hebrew names suggesting the family maintained Yahwistic religious identity despite Babylonian names. Ṭôḇiyyāhû = 'YHWH is good' — also a Yahwistic name, but he is designated haʿeḇeḏ hāʿammōnî, 'the Ammonite servant' (possibly a derogatory administrative title). Their response: wayyēraʿ lāhem, 'it was evil to them,' — displeasure at someone seeking good for Israel.
11: wāʾāḇôʾ yᵉrûšālaim wāʾehî šām yāmîm šᵉlōšāh: 'and I came to Jerusalem and was there three days.' The three-day waiting period before action is a biblical narrative convention marking time for transition, orientation, and preparation (cf. Joshua 1:11; Ezra 8:15). Nehemiah does not immediately convene the leaders or present his commission — he waits, observes, and assesses privately before any public announcement. This deliberate delay reflects strategic thinking: the opposition (Sanballat, Tobiah) is already known to be present, and premature disclosure would allow countermeasures.
12: waʾāqûm laylāh ʾanî waʾănāšîm mᵉʿaṭ: 'and I arose by night, I and a few men.' The night inspection is a strategic secrecy measure. The key phrase: wᵉʾet-habᵉhēmāh ʾašer ʾanî rōḵēḇ ʿāleyhā, 'and the animal on which I was riding' — singular, suggesting only Nehemiah had a mount while the others went on foot. Most importantly: wᵉlōʾ-higgadtî lᵉʾāḏām mah-ʾĕlōhay nōtēn ʾel-libbî laʿăśôt lîrûšālāim, 'and I did not tell anyone what my God was putting in my heart to do for Jerusalem.' libbî (my heart) as the seat of divine motivation — YHWH places the purpose (nōtēn, gives, puts) in the interior person; Nehemiah receives the mission as a God-given burden before any human strategy is formulated.
13: wāʾēṣēʾ bᵉšaʿar haggebay laylāh: 'and I went out by the Valley Gate at night.' The inspection route goes counterclockwise around the southern portion of the city wall. ṯannîn is the Jackal's Well (some translations 'Serpent's Well'; ṯannîn can mean jackal or sea monster, and the toponym is uncertain). The condition of the walls: ûbᵉšāʿārāyw ʾūḵlāh ʾēš, 'and the gates had been devoured by fire.' The verb ʾāḵal (eat, devour) used for the fire's destruction is vivid — the city was consumed. This is eyewitness memoir language preserving the specific emotional experience of seeing the ruins firsthand.
14: wāʾeʿᵉḇōr ʾel-šaʿar hāʾašpōt: 'and I passed on to the Dung Gate.' The Dung Gate (šaʿar hāʾašpōt — ʾašpōt from ʾašpāh, dunghill or refuse heap) was located at the southern tip of the city near the Hinnom Valley, where waste was disposed. The condition Nehemiah found: wᵉʾên-māqôm labbᵉhēmāh laʿăḇōr taḥtāy, 'and there was no place for the animal under me to pass.' The rubble was so extensive the mount could not navigate it — Nehemiah had to dismount and inspect on foot. This physical detail authenticates the memoir's eyewitness character.
15: wāʾeĕhî ʿōleh bannāḥal laylāh: 'and I went up by the Kidron Valley at night.' After the Dung Gate, Nehemiah turned back through the Valley/Kidron, inspecting the eastern wall section. wāʾāšûḇ waʾāḇôʾ bᵉšaʿar haggay: 'and I turned back and entered through the Valley Gate.' The inspection was cut short by the impassability of the southern section. The route covered reveals which portions of the wall were most severely damaged: the southern and southwestern sections near the Dung Gate.
16: wᵉhassᵉgānîm lōʾ yāḏᵉʿû ʾānāh hālaḵtî: 'and the officials did not know where I had gone.' sᵉgānîm (officials, prefects) are the local administrative leaders. The secrecy is maintained even from sympathetic leaders. Three categories of leaders are listed: sᵉgānîm (officials), hiyhûḏîm (Jews generally), kōhănîm (priests), ḥōrîm (nobles), and sᵉgānîm again in the Nehemiah 2:17 address. The careful enumeration of the social hierarchy reflects the need to unite all sectors of Judean society behind the project.
17: wāʾōmar ʾălêhem ʾattem rōʾîm hārāʿāh ʾašer ʾănaḥnû ʾûhă: 'and I said to them, you see the trouble in which we are.' The pronoun shift is significant: Nehemiah uses 'we' (ʾănaḥnû) throughout — he identifies himself completely with the community he came to serve. The diagnosis: Jerusalem is desolate (ḥārēḇāh), its gates burned. The appeal: lᵉḵû wᵉniḇneh ʾet-ḥômat yᵉrûšālaim, 'come, let us build the wall of Jerusalem.' The hortatory cohortative (niḇneh) invites collective ownership of the project. The shame argument: wᵉlōʾ nihyeh ʿôḏ ḥerpāh, 'that we may no longer be a reproach.' ḥerpāh (shame, reproach) is the motivating term — the community's honor is bound up with the city's defenses.
18: wāʾaggîḏ lāhem ʾet-yaḏ ʾĕlōhay ʾăšer-hāyᵉṯāh ṭôḇāh ʿālay: 'and I told them of the hand of my God that was good upon me.' yaḏ ʾĕlōhîm (the hand of God) as a formula of divine favor and enablement appears seven times in Ezra-Nehemiah (Ezra 7:6, 9, 28; Nehemiah 8:18, 22, 31; Nehemiah 2:8, 18). The formula balances divine initiative with human action: YHWH's good hand is evident in the royal authorization Nehemiah received. The community's response: wayyaḥazzᵉqû yᵉdêhem laṭṭôḇāh, 'and they strengthened their hands for the good work' — the ḥāzaq idiom (strengthen/encourage) will become the dominant verb in the wall-building narrative of Nehemiah 3.
19: wayyiśmaʿ sanballat haḥōrōnî wᵉṭôḇiyyāh hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿammōnî wāgeśem hāʿarāḇî: 'Sanballat the Horonite, Tobiah the Ammonite servant, and Geshem the Arab heard.' A new opponent: Geshem the Arab (gašmû in Nehemiah 6:6) — archaeological evidence identifies a Qedarite Arab confederacy with a leader named Gashm who controlled the territory south and east of Judah ca. 445 BCE (a silver bowl inscription from the eastern Nile Delta reads qyn br gšm mlk qdr, 'Qainu son of Geshem, king of Qedar'). The triple opposition represents the three geographic threats: north (Sanballat/Samaria), east (Tobiah/Ammon), south (Geshem/Arabia). Their tactic: wayyalegû bānû wayyiḇzû, 'they mocked us and despised us,' then the accusation of rebellion.
20: wāʾāšîḇ ʾōtām dāḇār: 'and I replied to them.' The triple exclusion formula that follows is one of Nehemiah's most rhetorically forceful statements: (1) ʾĕlōhê haššāmayim hûʾ yaṣlîaḥ lānû, 'the God of heaven will make us prosper'; (2) waʾănaḥnû ʿăḇāḏāyw nāqûm ûḇānînû, 'and we his servants will arise and build'; (3) the exclusion itself — wᵉlakem ʾên-ḥēleq wᵉṣᵉḏāqāh ûzikkārôn bîrûšālāim, 'but you have no portion, right, or memorial in Jerusalem.' Three Hebrew terms: ḥēleq (portion/share — legal stake in the community), ṣᵉḏāqāh (righteousness/right — legitimate claim), zikkārôn (memorial/remembrance — historical continuity). The triple exclusion is a legal formula barring the opponents from any claim on Jerusalem's future — they have no stake, no right, and no history in the city.
